content: Does anyone know off the top of their head which config option in the Linux kernel seems to cause insn_decoder_test to fail? I'm working off of the patch set for rnull by Andreas based on the 6.8 kernel and this problem popped up, but for some reason I was not bumping into it on rust-next.
Can you paste the error you get?
Is it during build or boot?

the error is specifically:

5ddb0
make[2]: *** [arch/x86/tools/Makefile:26: posttest] Error 3
make[1]: *** [arch/x86/Makefile:284: bzImage] Error 2
make[1]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....

The only reason I'm asking specifically for a config option is because this didn't seem to be present on one branch and was on another. I want to ensure that I know what to disable when switching branches.
.config is what I am using to build. Maybe you can cross check.
I'll take a look. Also is there no way to edit on Zulip? I think the build error I pasted got clipped somehow.

Sorry about that, it was missing the actual cause of the Make failure. This is the complete build error:

arch/x86/tools/insn_decoder_test: error: malformed line 2463022:
5ddb0
make[2]: *** [arch/x86/tools/Makefile:26: posttest] Error 3
make[1]: *** [arch/x86/Makefile:284: bzImage] Error 2
make[1]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....

Just as an FYI, the way I developed my config (since I'm working on a Fedora VM) is I just copied the Fedora config file from the installed kernel and modified the bits I needed to to enable Rust. Is there generally a better way of doing this or does this sound reasonable?

That is a good approach when you want to have a kernel close to your distribution's, yeah.

For development, you may want a simpler/small one to speed up links or full rebuilds, i.e. disabling as many things you don't need.
Thanks for the report @John Baublitz!
By the way, before you change your setup/config, please save the kernel commit hash/branch and the .config you used so that we can try to reproduce it and minimize it.

@Miguel Ojeda I saved the config file that's failing but since this is occuring on a patch set branch and not an upstream branch, should I also test this on the upstream 6.8 branch to see if the same thing happens there?
Yeah, please. I would recommend checking on mainline (Linus' branch) with Rust enabled, and if that also fails, then without Rust enabled (to check if it is related to Rust).
Thanks a lot!
Sure thing! I'll let you know what I find.
@John Baublitz I talked a bit about minimal configurations here. If you scroll up a bit I posted some configs.

For development, I would not advise building a full distro config. It takes forever, and most things does not matter for what you are doing. For development I only enable what I need to boot Debian in qemu. I boot Debian on real hardware for performance tests, and then I base my config on upstream Debian config. But I remove a lot of modules from the upstream config. The kernel build system has a feature where it can disable all modules not loaded on the running system, or based on stored output of lsmod. It's the localmodconfig make target.

@Andreas Hindborg localmodconfig is exactly what I'm looking for, thanks. Because @Miguel Ojeda is interested and this error has the potential to be Fedora config specific so this would likely be benefical for Fedora to be aware of too if they enable the Rust stuff, I'm going to do two things. I'm still going to test with this bloated config on the regular upstream 6.8 patch series, then with the localmodconfig config and see if I can reproduce the problem on either or both. If I can, I'll try to narrow down exactly what it is so we can potentially document it based on what your stipped down config is missing that mine has.
Thanks John, if you can find the culprit config option/commit/branch, that would be awesome.

Okay so I can reproduce it with this
config
on the v6.8-r4 tag of the Github mirror (841c35169323cd833294798e58b9bf63fa4fa1de).
I'm going to try the minifying command that Andreas suggested now.
Good news! I was able to reproduce it on the same tag with this minified config. @Andreas Hindborg I'm about to crossreference it with yours to see what might be going on.

@Miguel Ojeda Okay so I found at least some interesting information with my first disable and it's not great. I saw you didn't have CONFIG_DEBUG_INFO=y enabled in your config, @Andreas Hindborg and noticed this mainly because of your pahole version being 0 and mine being set. Since pahole is called out in actual config option for Rust support itself, I disabled DEBUG just to try it out and it now compiles. :melting_face:
I build with debug from time to time, no problems encountered
There was something about pahole not working with Rust at one point https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/20230111152050.559334-1-yakoyoku@gmail.com/
I think it was just disabled for Rust

I don't think it's specifically the DEBUG option but whatever is enabled in my kernel with DEBUG I can say definitively causes a compilation failure and when DEBUG is turned off, it compiles successfully. I also noticed that in my config, pahole exclude appears to be enabled (as is required for Rust support), so pahole might be turned off altogether for Rust which is why I'm not saying it's definitively pahole. It may just be a distraction, but I felt like it might be worth mentioning.
This config diff is the diff between the minified config that works and the one that fails. The config on the left works and the one on the right fails.

@Andreas Hindborg I confirmed (as I suspected just because someone likely would've reported it before me) that it is not the debug config option but something toggled off by the debug option that's causing this. I'm going to work on testing each option and see if I can figure out what specifically is causing this.

@Andreas Hindborg Can you manually enable just the CONFIG_X86_DECODER_SELFTEST option? I've noticed that if you turn off debug and then turn it back on, it disables the test that's failing. I'm curious if, on your current config for 6.8-r4, the test fails. There's also this which means someone has at the very least noticed it before.
Actually, never mind, I'll test it out. I have your config so I can easily try.

CC @Miguel Ojeda I've confirmed that enabling this test on all of the configs I've tested results in the test failing. Not sure exactly what the problem is here, but it seems like it may be legitimate.
Maybe the test is incompatible with code segments emitted by rustc?

If I see it correctly, there are two tests that get enabled by this config

# TEST    posttest
  llvm-objdump -d -j .text ./vmlinux | awk -f ./arch/x86/tools/objdump_reformat.awk | arch/x86/tools/insn_decoder_test -y -v
arch/x86/tools/insn_decoder_test: success: Decoded and checked 6984988 instructions
# TEST    posttest
  arch/x86/tools/insn_sanity -y -m 1000000
arch/x86/tools/insn_sanity: Success: decoded and checked 1000000 random instructions with 0 errors (seed:0x874098c4)

They both pass on my machine and config (the branch is essentially rust-next). Could you maybe find out if it is the first or second test failing? You can also run the commands manually, no need to rebuild each time, maybe also worth raising the limit on the second test to check all insns.

Ah, I see you already posted arch/x86/tools/insn_decoder_test: error: malformed line 2463022: above, then it might be worth checking the corresponding line in llvm-objdump -d -j .text ./vmlinux | awk -f ./arch/x86/tools/objdump_reformat.awk.
You may be able to use llvm-addr2line to get the source line for the failing insn.

@Valentin Obst So I feel like I must be missing something very obvious because:

[jbaublitz@fedora linux]$ objdump -d vmlinux | awk -f ./arch/x86/tools/objdump_reformat.awk | ./arch/x86/tools/insn_decoder_test 2>&1 | wc -l
1208945

Those are literally all warnings.
Hmmmm this may actually need some more attention. I can reproduce it on 6.8-r4 but not on rust-next so there's definitely something there. I'm using the same config in both cases.
arch/x86/tools/ins_decoder_test.c has some options to enable more debug output. Perhaps you can enable those?
Probably enabling a kernel with Rust enabled has some things in the ELF image that these scripts do not expect

So, I can reproduce the issue on 6.8-rc5. From what I can tell, the issue is that in insn_decoder_test there is a hard-coded max line length of 256 chars, that is exceeded by some rust symbol names, which in turn causes the parsing to be confused. This was already pointed out a year ago here but seemingly not addressed.
Probably something like KSYM_NAME_LEN+X would be appropriate, or have the awk script emit a line with the max line length and then allocate a buffer dynamically.
Rewrite the program in Rust and just use a Vec or something instead of a static buffer :laughing:
On a more serious note, I think it is a good idea to pick up that patch and go with KSYM_NAME_LEN+whatever.

Valentin Obst said:

Probably something like KSYM_NAME_LEN+X would be appropriate, or have the awk script emit a line with the max line length and then allocate a buffer dynamically.

Hi,
sorry, I was not following this topic in depth, but I think this was addressed in here: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20231119180145.157455-1-sergio.collado@gmail.com/
And the solution adopted, was the propoused by David, here: https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/Y9ES4UKl%2F+DtvAVS@gmail.com/T/#m3ef0e12bb834d01ed1ebdcae12ef5f2add342077
Okay, great, so its just a matter of this patch being merged :+1:
@Sergio González Collado thanks. The fix to the instruction decode test could probably be sent alone right? Does it depend on the unit test?
Yeah, it would be best to send the fix alone, and then the test can be taken after that.
I am contacting David and Masami to see if they would be OK with that.
Sure, no problem, I can try to do it today. I had already contancted David about it when I wrote the patch, but please check it.
Ah, that is great, thanks Sergio. Masami originally wrote the test, so my idea was to see if he would like to give it an Acked-by for Ingo to pick it up or similar.

Haha @Andreas Hindborg we've come full circle. It was the symbol length after all! @Sergio González Collado That was the very first patch I saw when I was Googling this but wasn't sure if it was related.
For now, I'll disable the test and get to work on picking up the patches for devicemapper that were submitted.

John Baublitz said:

Valentin Obst So I feel like I must be missing something very obvious because:

[jbaublitz@fedora linux]$ objdump -d vmlinux | awk -f ./arch/x86/tools/objdump_reformat.awk | ./arch/x86/tools/insn_decoder_test 2>&1 | wc -l
1208945

Those are literally all warnings.

Concerning all those warnings, they are not rly explained by the buffer size, right? Just guessing, but I think it might be because you used GNU objdump on a kernel compiled with LLVM? (Or it might be because you did not limit it to the .text section)
Ah that is a fair point, let me retry with LLVM objdump and see.

@Valentin Obst 

[jbaublitz@fedora linux]$ llvm-objdump -d vmlinux | awk -f ./arch/x86/tools/objdump_reformat.awk | ./arch/x86/tools/insn_decoder_test 2>&1 | wc -l
3126980

so it may actually be due to symbol length.
Hm, what about the second thing, i.e., only disassemble the .text section?
2 messages were moved from this topic to #Help > SocketCAN by Miguel Ojeda.
